title: Rescue Me 🛟
description: Catch errors gracefully before they can terminate your code.

slides.title: The Safety Net 🕸️
slides.content:
We don't want our whole app to instantly die just because one user typed a bad number.

To prevent the crash, we must catch the error as it falls. We do this using a `begin...rescue` block!

slides.title: Begin and Rescue
slides.content:
You wrap your dangerous code inside `begin`.

If it works perfectly, the program ignores `rescue` and keeps going. But if the dangerous code crashes, Ruby immediately jumps down into the `rescue` block instead of dying!

```ruby
begin
  # Dangerous code here
rescue
  # Fallback code here
end
```

slides.title: A Graceful Recovery
slides.content: Run this! The division by zero still fails. But instead of the program terminating, it gracefully falls perfectly into the `rescue` block and prints the warning, allowing the script to beautifully finish!
slides.codeSnippet:
puts "1. Starting..."

begin
  result = 10 / 0 # Crash!
rescue
  puts "WARNING: You cannot divide by zero!"
end

puts "2. Finished! Look, we survived!"

quizData.question: Which keyword acts as the safety net, essentially saying 'If the code above me crashes, run this code instead of dying'?
quizData.explanation: If the `begin` block generates an Exception, it is instantly passed down into the `rescue` block to be handled.

slides.title: Catching Specifics 🔍
slides.content:
A naked `rescue` catches absolutely ALL errors (almost).

But what if you brilliantly only want to rescue from a `ZeroDivisionError`, and you *want* to let standard `TypeError`s crash the app?

You can explicitly specify what your net catches: `rescue ZeroDivisionError`

slides.title: Named Rescue
slides.content: Run this! We try to rescue a `ZeroDivisionError`, but the code actually triggers a `TypeError`. Because our net was the wrong shape, the program crashes anyway!
slides.codeSnippet:
begin
  puts "Adding string and number..."
  puts 5 + "Hello"
rescue ZeroDivisionError
  puts "We saved you from a math error!"
end

quizData.question: What happens if a `NoMethodError` occurs inside a block that specifically strictly says `rescue TypeError`?

options.text: The error passes straight through the net and crashes the program.
It converts the error cleanly into a TypeError.

quizData.explanation: By explicitly naming the error class, you tell Ruby 'ONLY rescue this specific type of object. Let everything else die'.

slides.title: Inspecting the Damage 🔬
slides.content: You can capture the actual Exception object itself to examine it by using a rocket: `rescue => e`. Then you can `puts e.message` to print the exact reason for the crash!
